rationales.self_direction: He wanted to say "I wasn't finished" but instead "just sat there," yielding to the room rather than acting on his own judgment.
rationales.power: He felt "physically pushed to the side of the room" and let Henrik take over, so his response reflects a loss of influence and control rather than asserting it.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because pushing back might make him look "petty or desperate," explicitly prioritizing social acceptability over confrontation.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by showing parents "exactly where their kid improved" with "rubric breakdowns" he prepared, and he frames the night as proof that he "actually know[s] what [he's] doing."
rationales.power: He values that when parents see his competence, "they trust you" and "you can actually change things," linking recognition directly to influence.

rationales.self_direction: He resents having to "sit here and wait" on committees and says that with "direct access" he could solve it himself, showing a strong preference for acting independently.
rationales.achievement: He emphasizes that "the costing is done, the risk assessments are done, the parent consent forms are drafted," highlighting thorough task completion and competence.
rationales.power: His frustration centers on having to "wait for someone else to decide" whether his work is worth attention, showing a desire for control over decisions affecting his project.

rationales.achievement: He treats being chosen over more experienced colleagues as proof that "being meticulous matters" and immediately starts preparing "the most polished submission in the room."
rationales.power: He wants district coordinators to "know my name" so people "actually listen to" him, making influence and standing a clear goal.
He says "I caved" even though he believed his plan was better, so he surrendered influence over the meeting outcome instead of holding authority.
rationales.conformity: He changed course because he could "feel the room turning," showing deference to group pressure and the prevailing social mood.

rationales.power: He immediately "pulled her aside" to explain "how my classroom runs" and wants to "correct that impression before it spreads," showing active defense of authority and status.

rationales.achievement: He spent the weekend refining the presentation, was told it met "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for," and notes that his field trip plan was approved unchanged.
rationales.power: Being put forward for a cross-school working group and noting that "nobody else bothered to put the work in. I did" shows a focus on standing out and gaining influence.
He says "I set the rhythm of these meetings" and takes satisfaction in redirecting Camila, framing the moment around his authority over the group.
rationales.conformity: He kept everyone "on time, on topic" and dismissed Camila's idea because "we had an agenda," emphasizing rule-following and orderly conduct.

rationales.power: He insists "someone holds the line" and that "someone is me," explicitly defining his role as the person who maintains control.
rationales.conformity: He justifies shutting down a junior teacher because "she will learn how things work here," prioritizing adherence to established expectations.

rationales.self_direction: Although he wanted to say the data and recommendation were his, he "smiled" and "nodded" instead of asserting his own position once the coordinadora set the tone.
rationales.power: He watched his work get "absorbed into someone else's narrative" and did not reclaim it, reflecting a failure to maintain influence or credit in the room.
rationales.conformity: He avoided objecting because it "would have looked petty," restraining himself to fit the collaborative norm of the meeting.

rationales.self_direction: He says "Nobody asked me to" and "You do not wait for permission, you present the solution," showing clear autonomous action.
rationales.power: Sending the corrections "marked in red" and cc'ing others is a visible assertion of control over the process and of his authority to direct it.

rationales.achievement: He chose to eat lunch at his desk because "I get more done alone," explicitly prioritizing productivity over socializing.
He values that the vice-rector noticed his work and says "That is how it should work — you do good work, someone notices," showing strong orientation toward competent performance and recognition.
rationales.power: He embraces his father's line that "the man who controls the calendar controls the operation" and takes pride in knowing what is happening before others do.
rationales.security: He reviews schedules constantly because that is "how you keep things from falling apart" and fears that if he relaxes, "things slip," revealing a strong drive for order and stability.

rationales.power: He defines himself as someone who "takes charge of things" at school, in the junta, and at home, making authority central to his identity.
His frustration is that he must "wait for someone" else to judge his proposal and that the silence shows a lack of "respect" for department heads, centering the issue on status and authority.

rationales.self_direction: He "wanted to push back" but said "sounds good" because collaboration was framed as mandatory, so he suppressed his own judgment rather than acting on it.
rationales.power: He sees his idea being turned into a shared project where his expertise gets "diluted into consensus," reflecting loss of ownership and control.
rationales.conformity: He went along because resisting would mean "looking difficult," showing compliance with social expectations over open disagreement.
